title: Neanderthals Used Birch Tar as Medicine
summary: Neanderthals were ancient people who lived long ago. They used birch tar, a sticky substance from birch trees, not just as glue for tools but also as medicine. Birch tar helped them fight infections from cuts and wounds. Modern Indigenous groups like the Mi'kmaq in Canada still use birch tar for healing. Scientists tested birch tar against bacteria and found it can stop some germs, like Staphylococcus aureus, which causes skin infections. This shows Neanderthals were smart and knew how to use natural things to stay healthy. They made birch tar by heating birch bark, and it worked like an antiseptic to clean wounds.

background_read: Neanderthals were early humans who lived in Europe and Asia thousands of years ago. They used tools and fire, and this article shows they also used natural remedies like birch tar to treat injuries. Birch tar comes from birch trees, which are common in cold regions. Indigenous peoples today still use similar methods, showing how old knowledge can be helpful. Understanding this helps us see Neanderthals as smart and resourceful, not just primitive.
Article_Structure: WHO: Neanderthals and modern Indigenous groups like the Mi'kmaq
WHAT: Used birch tar as glue and medicine to fight infections
WHERE: In ancient Europe and modern Canada
WHY: To heal wounds and stay healthy, showing Neanderthals were intelligent

perspectives.description: Neanderthals had advanced knowledge of natural remedies.
Tests confirm birch tar's antibacterial properties.
